Project Nexus -- Architecture & Data Dependencies

Architecture Establishment (v2)   |   Prepared by Matt Burlage   |   Date: 2026-05-13   |   Audience: Project leads and workers
Status of this document. Everything below is the pre-project timeline design of the Nexus Architecture.
CONFIRMED decision recorded with an owner   |   Draft coordinator's working assumption -- needs validation   |   OPEN QUESTION unresolved -- needs an owner to decide   |   INDUSTRY-STANDARD drawn from common life-insurance practice
Glossary. The document is read by actuarial, finance, IT, and governance audiences; abbreviations are defined here for convenience.
  • ACF -- Actual Cashflows. Realized cash flows from the period just closed (premiums collected, claims paid, surrenders, commissions, etc.). Sourced from Coreflexi via the Data Lake; consumed by Moody's 2.x for experience-variance analysis against ECF.
  • ALM -- Asset-Liability Management. Pairing liability cash flows with asset projections to optimise capital, reserves, and risk.
  • AML / CFT -- Anti-Money Laundering / Combating the Financing of Terrorism (regulatory regime applicable to Governance).
  • CDC -- Change Data Capture (one-way replication from the production Core Admin DB into the Data Lake's Raw zone).
  • CoA / GL -- Chart of Accounts / General Ledger. Mastered in Sun 2.x.
  • Core Administration Database (Core Admin DB) -- the production database owned by Coreflexi 2.x (sits outside Project Nexus). Authoritative store of policy, customer, premium, claim, lapse, and related administration records. Feeds the Data Lake one-way as a non-production replica (CDC / batch); the Data Lake never writes back to it until production tests are completed.
  • Data Lake -- the central, non-production data platform that every Project Nexus module reads from and writes to. Four zones: Raw / Standardised / Curated / Reporting snapshot (Section 4.1). Holds PII-masked data only; PII stays in the Core Admin DB upstream and is masked en route via the daily sync.
  • Data Wiki -- the documentation surface built on top of the Data Lake and MDM; consumed by all Claude Code agents on the programme.
  • ECF -- Expected Cashflows. Projected cash flows produced by the actuarial engine (Cashfluid) over the projection horizon, given current assumption sets. Feeds Moody's 2.x for HKFRS17 measurement (LRC, CSM).
  • ESG -- in this document refers to Economic Scenario Generator, not Environmental / Social / Governance. Produces stochastic and deterministic scenarios for actuarial / capital projections.
  • HKRBC -- the Hong Kong Risk-Based Capital regime (live July 2024) administered by HKIA (Hong Kong Insurance Authority). Has three pillars: P1 quantitative (PCR / MCR = Prescribed / Minimum Capital Requirement), P2 governance (ORSA = Own Risk & Solvency Assessment), P3 disclosure.
  • HKFRS17 -- the Hong Kong accounting standard for insurance contracts (equivalent to IFRS 17). CSM (Contractual Service Margin), RA (Risk Adjustment), BBA / VFA / PAA (the three measurement models) and OCI (Other Comprehensive Income) are sub-concepts.
  • Lake Functions -- data manipulations performed centrally by the Data Lake on behalf of the Project Nexus modules. Covers generic data work (ingestion, deduplication, MDM survivorship, code-list harmonisation, lineage capture, access governance, generic aggregations, etc.) that is reusable across multiple modules. The default home for any overlapping work.
  • LoC -- Letter of Credit (one of the reinsurance instruments flagged for the future reinsurance discussion).
  • MDM -- Master Data Management. The governance discipline for canonical entities (Customer, Policy, Product, Counterparty, Chart of Accounts, Currency, Calendar) within the Data Lake. Section 4.2 lays out the components.
  • Module Functions -- domain-specific data manipulations performed inside an individual Project Nexus module (Cashfluid, Sun 2.x, Moody's 2.x, Transactional, HKRBC Reporting, Governance) because they require specialist actuarial, accounting, regulatory, or transactional business logic.
  • MPL -- MyPace Life Limited, the Hong Kong licensed insurance carrier.
  • Nexus Module -- a component that sits inside the Project Nexus boundary (Data Lake, Cashfluid, Sun 2.x, Moody's 2.x, Transactional, HKRBC Reporting, Governance, Compliance, Strategy). Distinct from external "systems" such as Coreflexi 2.x / the Core Admin DB, which sit outside Project Nexus.
  • PDPO -- Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (Hong Kong's privacy law).
  • SAA -- Strategic Asset Allocation. The longer-term Cashfluid workstream (Phase H) that optimises asset mix against reserve / capital requirements.
  • VDR -- Virtual Data Room (used by the Transactional system for M&A and Section 24 deal materials).
Contents
  1. Project Nexus at a glance (diagram)
  2. Architectural topology & open questions
  3. The ten modules -- brief descriptions
  4. Data Lake -- Architecture and Functions
  5. Data dependencies -- inputs, outputs, manipulations
  6. Next steps
  7. Sample timelines and dependency mapping

1. Project Nexus at a glance

A preliminary visual overview of the system. Matt Burlage is putting together a more detailed pure dataflow (with much help from Shanshan's prior work).

Outside Nexus boundaries

  • Coreflexi 2.x and Core Admin DB sit outside Nexus. Coreflexi 2.x and the Core Admin DB sit outside the Project Nexus boundary, as does the External Data Sources block.
  • Data flows into the Data Lake are one-way and non-production. The Core Admin DB feeds the Data Lake one-way (a non-production replica -- the production database is not compromised), while Coreflexi 2.x feeds its non-live data into the Data Lake as work progresses.
  • External data bypasses the Data Lake for data-protection reasons. Vendor / market / regulator feeds (market data, FX, ESG, mortality / lapse studies, regulatory text, sanctions lists, etc.) are routed directly to the consuming module rather than landing in the Data Lake, so that licence-restricted, PII-bearing, or otherwise sensitive third-party data does not accumulate in the shared analytics plane.
  • External data routes via Flow 1 (direct-to-module) when the source provides a stable API / structured feed consumed by one (or a small handful of) modules, and via Flow 2 (External Data Ingestion Module → Data Lake → modules) when the data is ad-hoc, multi-consumer, or quality-variable. The Ingestion Module (owned by the Data Lake team) is the single common-validation / MDM-alignment layer for Flow 2; data-protection isolation is preserved by the core rule that no external source ever has direct read/write access to a Nexus database. See the Confirmed decision "External data: two flows, one core rule" below and Section 4.6.
  • Data Lake mediates everything inside Nexus. The Data Lake (with MDM and the Data Wiki) sits at the top of Nexus, and every interaction within Nexus is mediated by it; inter-system arrows are drawn directly between systems for readability. Section 4 details the Data Lake's architecture and Lake Functions. Numbers (1-8) match the system descriptions in Section 3.

Inside Nexus

  • Data Lake sits at the top. Every interaction below is mediated by it, but, as mentioned, the inter-system arrows are drawn directly between systems to keep the picture readable.
  • Inner workings. Section 4 lays out what actually happens inside the Data Lake.

Dependencies

  • Diagram intentionally bypasses the Data Lake for readability. For the purpose of understanding dependencies, the diagram has not been set up in this way (i.e. all data routed through the Data Lake).
  • All dependencies drawn are preliminary. As with other features in the document, the dependencies drawn are purely preliminary and will change upon further input.

2. Architectural topology & open questions

Project Nexus aims to design and develop PACE Solution Limited's accounting, actuarial, finance, transactional and governance system architecture. The high-level points below outline the core confirmed decision points and key open questions.

Please review accordingly.

Confirmed decisions [by Matt Burlage, feel free to challenge]

  • CONFIRMED Hub-and-spoke via the Data Lake. All inter-system data flows route through the Data Lake. Point-to-point integrations between operational systems are disallowed by default; the Data Lake is the single integration backbone and the enforcement point for MDM. The diagram in Section 1 draws inter-system arrows directly only for readability -- the physical flow is always via the Lake.
  • CONFIRMED Computation stress points must be identified and prioritized, especially with regards to interdependent calculations. This is incredibly important because, for example, our competitive advantage in ALM will come from configurable and rapid recalculation of asset allocations and reserve impacts. All data dependencies the ALM has in such calculations should be understood and optimized so as to not create a bottleneck in the pipeline.
  • CONFIRMED Core Admin DB is outside Project Nexus. The Core Admin DB is the upstream production database. It feeds the Data Lake one-way as a non-production replica (CDC or batch); the Data Lake never writes back to the production DB until all necessary production tests are completed.
  • CONFIRMED All inputs and outputs must be thoroughly understood before broader system developments continue.
  • CONFIRMED All project plans need to clearly lay out where the human-in-the-loop validation step is. For example, with Cashfluid, there will be a parity check dashboard to ensure all legacy model outputs match new model outputs, and an actuary sign-off checklist will be generated. For the governance layer, a chief compliance officer, or someone, will need to validate that all the structured data extracted from a given policy or HKIA Guideline are comprehensive.
  • CONFIRMED All projects will undergo tech-reviews performed by Kai Wang, Luke Lai, or Mark Chen. All project leads should bake this into their respective project timelines.
  • CONFIRMED Platform: MySQL-first curated analytics platform. The Data Lake runs on a MySQL-first stack with Python pipelines and GitLab for code, mappings, MDM definitions and Wiki. Modules live on the same MySQL platform in their own schemas; module logic is Python, reading and writing through controlled interfaces. Azure migration is deferred and revisited only when MySQL storage / query concurrency, semi-structured volume, or orchestration complexity force it.
  • CONFIRMED Reporting marts placement. Reporting marts live in Governance as the single front-end / access surface from which final reports are downloaded (statutory packs, management reporting, HKFRS17 disclosure mart, HKRBC dashboards, board / ALCO packs). The underlying reporting-data snapshots (the official data used for each published submission, e.g. a final HKRBC return) live in the Data Lake's Reporting snapshot zone (Section 4.1) so every figure can be reconciled back to source.
  • CONFIRMED External data: two flows, one core rule. Core rule: no external source has direct read or write access to a Nexus-controlled database. External providers never get a connection string to Coreflexi, the Data Lake, or any module store. All external data enters Nexus via one of two controlled channels:
    • Flow 1 -- Direct-to-Module Feed. For external sources that publish via a stable API or professionally-structured feed where quality and schema are guaranteed at source (e.g., asset-manager projections, market data, regulator-published reference rates). The consuming Nexus module owns the inbound adapter and routes the data directly into the module, bypassing the Lake. Chosen for build efficiency (module owns its own connector against a stable contract) and processing efficiency (no Lake round-trip for data already curated at source). Some runs may require this on-demand for a single run.
    • Flow 2 -- External Data Ingestion Module → Data Lake → Consuming Modules. For ad-hoc, multi-consumer, or quality-variable data (regulatory documents, experience studies, sanctions / PEP lists, ESG reference, etc.). Data lands in the Lake's Raw zone via the dedicated Ingestion Module (Section 4.6), is validated, standardised, and promoted to Curated for cross-module consumption. Provides a single extraction-and-quality layer with versioning and lineage independent of any individual module build.
    Per-dataset routing decision: if the source provides a stable contract and only one or a small handful of modules consume it, use Flow 1; otherwise use Flow 2. The External Data Ingestion Module is owned by the Data Lake team (Section 4.6); per-dataset Flow 1 vs Flow 2 assignment is recorded in the MDM.
  • CONFIRMED Project lead vs. tech team workflow (build vs. review). Project leaders own the end-to-end functional build of their module; the tech team performs reviews and clean-up (architecture conformance, code quality, security, integration correctness, lineage and audit-trail hygiene). The split is: Kai Wang covers all upstream code (pre-Data-Lake -- Coreflexi, Core Admin DB extraction, source-system integrations), and Luke Lai covers all downstream code (Data Lake and everything inside the Project Nexus boundary). Project owners reach out at their own discretion when they feel their project needs review -- there is no enforced per-PR or per-gate cadence; the operating principle is that the project lead is responsible for engaging the relevant reviewer at sensible checkpoints.

Open architectural questions

  • OPEN QUESTION Per-module detailed architecture and project timeline. The full detailed architecture and project timeline for each module needs to be adequately completed by its respective project lead, so that inter-module handoffs, data contracts, and sequencing can be locked in across Project Nexus. All module timelines should aim to be completed by Monday 18 May 2026.
  • OPEN QUESTION Lake Function vs. Module Function distinction (per module). The working principle (generic / reusable data work = Lake Function; specialist business logic = Module Function; tie-break to the Lake on overlap) is laid out in Section 3 Card 2 and Section 4. Each module owner needs to confirm where the line sits for their module, with Shanshan, Michael and Matt Burlage (Data Lake / MDM leads):
    • Cashfluid (Matt Burlage / KJ). Lake supplies ACF, policyholder details, assumption sets, scenarios, investment data, reinsurance data, expense data; Cashfluid performs liability cash-flow projection, ESG runs, RA / CSM input generation, and stochastic cash-flow stress simulations (e.g. the 1,000 ESG-driven scenario runs that feed TVOG). Note that the GL36-prescribed Pillar 1 stress scenarios (mortality, longevity, life CAT, morbidity, expense, lapse) are applied separately inside HKRBC Reporting at the capital-aggregation layer, not in Cashfluid. Model-point grouping (clustering seriatim policies into representative model points for projection) sits in Cashfluid (future task). Expense allocation pre-aggregation (rolling raw GL expense data up into HKFRS17 cohort / product buckets before actuarial consumption) sits in the Data Lake (to be confirmed). Reinsurance gross/net split (applying treaty terms -- quota share, surplus retention, XoL -- to split each cash flow into gross / ceded / net legs) remains TBD.
    • Sun 2.x (Shanshan / Betty). Lake handles ingestion of upstream journal feeds (Coreflexi, Moody's 2.x, HKRBC Reporting, investment, market, expenses, reinsurance, intercompany), MDM CoA mapping resolution, journal-entry mapping logic, multi-GAAP conversion, FX translation, code-list harmonisation, lineage and snapshotting. Sun 2.x performs journal validation against CoA & posting rules, sub-ledger to GL postings, multi-currency re-translation, period-end close, allocations, consolidation, and fixed-asset / intercompany / tax cost-center allocation. Boundary on management reporting aggregations (statutory, financial statement, management reporting, planning / forecast) is the grey area.
    • Moody's 2.x (Betty / KJ / Yang Mingyang). Lake handles ingestion of Cashfluid measurement components (ECF, RA, CSM inputs), Sun 2.x expense / GL feeds, code-list harmonisation (product / contract groupings, FX), snapshotting of disclosure inputs and outputs, lineage capture from cash flow source through disclosure, and access governance on sensitive disclosure data. Moody's 2.x performs contract grouping, cohort assembly, CSM roll-forward, RA disclosure build, OCI option handling, presentation / aggregation to disclosure templates, statutory packaging, and HKFRS17 journal generation back into Sun 2.x. Boundary on segmental and group reporting (preliminary in scope) needs confirmation.
    • Transactional (Alex / Rui). Lake-mastered portfolio, counterparty and deal reference data vs. Transactional-side deal modelling, Section 24 carve-out logic, VDR / document agents, and transaction-specific scenario overlays.
    • HKRBC Reporting (KJ / Matt Burlage). Lake supplies actuarial inputs sourced from Cashfluid (best-estimate cash flow projections, 1,000 ESG scenarios, assumption sets, policyholder / contract data), plus asset / investment data, GL36 HKRBC discount curves, FX rates and reference calendars, and external HKIA regulatory templates ([CA.P.G.x] forms, GL36 prescribed stress definitions). HKRBC performs Pillar 1 end-to-end: BEL under GL36 (including TVOG via the 1,000-scenario stochastic run), PCR / MCR with the full PCA under the GL36 Section 10 modular approach (with diversification and prescribed stress scenarios), MOCE allocation, and [CA.P.G.x] regulatory return generation. Initial scope is Pillar 1 only; Pillar 2 (ORSA governance, stress orchestration) and Pillar 3 (public disclosure templates) placement is not yet settled and may sit more naturally inside the Governance module (Bill / Betty) -- this remains an OPEN QUESTION.
    • Governance (Bill / Betty / Yang Mingyang). Lake-mastered Risk Register, Model Risk inventory, Policy / Procedure inventory, Whistleblowing register, DOA matrices, Director / Resolutions registers, attestation status, and audit evidence. Governance-app-side workflow (board / committee packs, conduct workflow, audit coordination) still very much an OPEN QUESTION but with a slower timeline and a clear task of building out a suite of app ideas. Whether external feeds relevant to Governance (regulatory text, attestation benchmarks, etc.) should route via a single external-data ingestion layer rather than direct-to-module is an OPEN QUESTION (see Section 1, Outside Nexus boundaries).
    • Compliance (Betty / Yang Mingyang / Bill). Lake-mastered Obligation Register, Licence Register, Sanctions / PEP register and RPT register. Compliance-app-side workflow handles regulatory submissions (Monthly Attestation Program, Companies Ordinance filings, HKIA non-Pillar-1 returns, tax compliance), AML / CFT policy, and PDPO / data retention oversight. Boundary with HKRBC Reporting (Pillar 2 / 3 placement) remains an OPEN QUESTION. Compliance is the heaviest consumer of external feeds (sanctions lists, PEP lists, regulatory text, HKIA templates, tax updates), so whether these should arrive via a single external-data ingestion layer rather than module-direct is a particularly relevant OPEN QUESTION here (see Section 1, Outside Nexus boundaries).
    • Strategy (Matt Burlage / Bill / Betty). Lake supplies governed read access across all zones (Raw, Standardised, Curated and Reporting snapshot) plus MDM and Data Wiki context, so that a senior user can have AI-assisted ability to investigate any question end-to-end rather than being limited to pre-curated marts. Strategy module performs AI-enabled data exploration and business decision support (scenario analysis across Cashfluid / HKRBC / Sun outputs, cross-module decision framing) and houses the future FP&A capability. Shares a front-end / access surface with Governance and Compliance reporting marts.
  • OPEN QUESTION Coreflexi vs. Lake Function assignment (short-term reporting needs). The first and most pressing step is to understand which Lake Functions are needed, and for which reporting period, by Monday 18 May 2026. Once that list exists, each item can be triaged: do it immediately in Coreflexi (because the reporting deadline does not allow time to stand up the Lake Function), or implement it directly as a Lake Function. Implementing as a Lake Function is preferable from an architectural standpoint, but the decision ultimately turns on capacity -- who (Luke / Coreflexi, the Data Lake team, or elsewhere) actually has the bandwidth to build and operate each function within the required timeframe. Owners: Luke Lai (Coreflexi finance data), Shanshan Gu and Michael Alo (Data Lake), with sequencing coordinated with the module leads whose reporting periods are driving the demand.

3. The ten modules -- brief descriptions

Brief descriptions of each module with core points outlined.

1. Coreflexi 2.x -- Core Administration System Draft

Lead: WenboSupport: MarkStatus: In active build

Core policy administration system that owns the policy administration lifecycle and the Core Admin DB.

  • Data dictionary build. The data dictionary is being built and will live in a dedicated Data Wiki repo on the Coreflexi GitLab. Other projects can use this wiki repo to help Claude understand the system context, and additional project-specific wiki / documentation can be expanded there over time. The Coreflexi Data Wiki will be maintained separately from Project Nexus. For clarity, this will be separate from the Data Lake Data Wiki; however, the Data Lake Data Wiki will reference the Coreflexi Data Wiki to remain up to date on daily data updates.
  • No external inputs required currently. Coreflexi 2.x does not currently need external input integrations from Project Nexus modules.
  • Future Project Nexus build outputs. Future build outputs may be relied upon by various Project Nexus modules. Timelines for such to be aligned at a later date.
  • Short-term finance data taggings / functions over Lake Functions. Some finance data tagging and functions are currently done by Luke / Coreflexi. There are additional functions that otherwise would be classified as Lake Functions but are needed in the short term for immediate reporting periods and as such should be done in Coreflexi to ensure expedited progress. These need to be defined, as per the Open Question in Section 2.

2. Data Lake & MDM (with Data Wiki) Draft

Lead: Shanshan Gu & Michael AloWiki: Michael AloCore Admin DB docs: Kai WangStatus: New build

Single integration backbone for Project Nexus, governed by the Master Data Management (MDM) plan. Hosts the Core Admin DB extraction and subsequent manipulation / tagging / decomposition (the "Lake Functions") of the data for the various Project Nexus modules. In addition, the Data Lake will feature a Data Wiki, as previously described, which is intended to be consumed by all Claude Code agents building on this landscape.

As discussed with Shanshan and Alex and mentioned above, a key OPEN QUESTION for each module will be which data functions are classified as Lake Functions and which are performed by the various modules themselves (the "Module Functions"). Generally, any overlapping work should be a Lake Function. See Section 4.5 for more detail. The working principle proposed below (subject to refinement with each module owner):

3. Cashfluid -- Actuarial Liability Platform Draft

Lead: Matt BurlageSupport: KJ LinStatus: In active build

Liability cash-flow modelling for different blocks of acquired policies, including AI onboarding of such models. Goal is to ultimately cover best-estimate cash flows, assumption sets, projection runs, HKFRS17 measurement components (cash flows, discount curves, RA, CSM inputs) feeding Moody's 2.x for HKFRS17 measurement and disclosure, and feeding HKRBC Reporting with the actuarial inputs (best-estimate cash flows, 1,000 ESG scenarios, assumption sets) needed for Pillar 1 BEL / PCR / MCR computation. Many of these downstream models still need to be incorporated. On the assumptions side, experience studies can be added.

The first core expected computational load is the per-policy liability projections, which is currently being achieved at 0.1-0.2s per policy. This is the core first optimization step given all downstream ALM calculations and reporting calculations will rely on this.

Longer term future goal is to perform ALM scenario runs that optimize strategic asset allocation based on reserve requirement calculations, risk neutral ESG results, real world ESG results and capital market assumptions. Risk-neutral economic scenarios (ESG) are generated in-house; real-world asset projections will likely be sourced from external asset managers and ingested via the Data Lake.

Reinsurance is intentionally excluded from this draft pending discussion (see Next steps).

4. Sun 2.x (placeholder name) -- General Ledger Draft

Lead: Shanshan GuSupport: Betty PunStatus: In active build

Replaces the current Sun Systems for ledger / system of record / posting rules. Built as a single event-sourced GL with book as a first-class dimension on every JE line -- books covered are HKFRS (local GAAP), HKFRS17, HKRBC, Delta and Tax. Receives journal feeds from Coreflexi (premium / claim / commission), HKRBC Reporting (HKRBC Journal Entries), Moody's 2.x (HKFRS17 Journal Entries), as well as Expense Data and External Data. The Delta book is derived inside Sun 2.x as a book-to-book reconciliation (HKRBC − HKFRS17 reversing entries) once both HKFRS17 and HKRBC JEs have been posted, so Moody's 2.x and HKRBC Reporting remain independent and do not cross-read each other's outputs. Performs journal validation, multi-currency translation, period close, accruals, allocations, and consolidation of fixed assets, intercompany, tax, and company cost center allocations. See Section 5 for more.

5. Moody's 2.x (placeholder name) -- HKFRS17 CSM Calculation Engine and Subledger Draft

Lead: Betty Pun & KJ LinSupport: Matt BurlageStatus: In active build

Replaces current Moody's HKFRS17 tooling. The scope is CSM calculation engine, HKFRS17 expense allocation and subledger which will support disclosure & reporting. Data is sourced from the Data Lake through various sources: Core Admin DB (admin system), Sun 2.x (expenses / general ledger), investment data (external spreadsheets from reinsurance and investment partners) and Cashfluid (ECF / Assumptions / RA). It produces HKFRS17 CSM initial recognition and roll-forward journal entries, financial-statement disclosures, and other reporting requirements (HKFRS17 specifically, while segmental and group reporting are preliminary in scope). See Section 5 for more.

6. Transactional -- Portfolio Transfers, Transaction Modelling, Document Generation Draft

Lead: Alex Moore & Rui HuangStatus: In active build
  • Deal evaluation and associated models: target summaries, forecast evaluation, returns analyses, and actuarial cash-flow sensitivities in coordination with Cashfluid.
  • Deal structuring: generation and evaluation of alternative structures (including Section 24 transfers and straight equity purchases) under various funding and reinsurance arrangements.
  • Synergy modules: expense scenario calculator, investment management, and par profit reallocation.
  • Deal process: due-diligence reports, transaction documentation markups, and NDA markups.
  • Deal governance: internal committee memos and valuation reports.

7. HKRBC Reporting Draft

Lead: KJ LinSupport: Matt BurlageStatus: In active build

HKRBC Reporting Module, scoped as a separate downstream consumer of Cashfluid actuarial outputs (best-estimate cash flows, ESG scenarios, assumption sets) as well as other modules. The HKRBC Reporting Module will own all Pillar 1 calculations end-to-end, specifically:

  • BEL (Best Estimate Liabilities) under the GL36 valuation basis, including subsequent MOCE allocation and TVOG (stochastic where required, using the 1,000 ESG scenarios).
  • PCR / MCR and the full PCA under the modular approach in GL36 Section 10, including diversification and the prescribed stress scenarios.
  • All [CA.P.G.x] regulatory return forms required for HKIA submission.

Pillar 2 (ORSA process, stress and reverse stress testing) and Pillar 3 (public disclosure templates) are also in the broader HKIA scope but the placement is not yet settled -- Pillar 2 in particular may sit more naturally inside the Governance module (Bill / Betty). Specific scope and process details for Pillars 2 / 3 to be clarified and defined. This has been flagged as an OPEN QUESTION.

8. Governance Draft

Lead: Bill Nichol & Betty PunSupport: Yang MingyangStatus: Not started

Internal governance backbone across the organisation, covering board / committee governance, conduct and ethics, operational governance, risk and capital governance, and the audit / attestation backbone.

  • Board & Committee Governance -- committee charters and calendars, board / ALCO / committee packs, resolutions register, director appointments and training, attestations and sign-offs.
  • Conduct & Ethics -- staff Code of Conduct, Fit & Proper, training and attestations, conduct / breach workflow, whistleblowing register, anti-bribery / gifts & entertainment register, complaints handling oversight.
  • Operational Governance -- policy / procedure management, Delegation of Authority (DOA) and approval matrices, procurement governance, third-party / outsourcing register, business continuity / disaster recovery governance, information security governance.
  • Risk & Capital Governance -- enterprise Risk Register, Risk Appetite Statement, ORSA governance, Model Risk Management, Capital Management Policy oversight, stress testing governance.
  • Audit & Attestation Backbone -- internal audit coordination, external audit support, attestation evidence repository (cross-cutting infrastructure supporting Governance, Compliance and Strategy).

See Section 5.8 for inputs / outputs / processes at sub-domain level.

9. Compliance Draft

Lead: Betty Pun & Yang MingyangSupport: Bill NicholStatus: Not started

External regulatory compliance -- discharging regulator-facing obligations across HKIA, HKFRS, AML / CFT, PDPO and adjacent frameworks. Owns the registers, submissions and policies that face out to regulators.

  • Regulatory Compliance -- Obligation Register (HKIA general + MPL-specific requirements and undertaking deliverables), licence management (HKIA insurer, ICB, HKFI, Intermediaries), Monthly Attestation Program, Companies Ordinance filings (Annual Return, SCR, AGM, statutory books), tax compliance oversight (profits tax, employer's return, agent / broker return), regulatory horizon scanning (new GLs, CIRs, consultation papers, IIC), AML / CFT policy oversight.
  • Data & Privacy -- PDPO / data privacy oversight, data retention and records management, sanctions / PEP screening governance.
  • RPT register -- related-party transactions register (cross-border flows with PSL and the planned IoM entity), feeding the Lake's tagging service and Sun 2.x's RPT dimension on every JE line.
  • Non-Pillar-1 HKIA returns -- composition, attestation, and filing workflow for the Quarterly Management Report, Annual Business Plan, RPT submissions and other HKIA returns that aren't Pillar 1 capital. Pulls data from HKRBC Reporting (capital), Sun 2.x (financials), Cashfluid (actuarial) via the reporting marts.

Pillar 2 (ORSA governance, stress orchestration) and Pillar 3 (public disclosure templates) placement remains an OPEN QUESTION across Governance / Compliance / Strategy.

10. Strategy Draft

Lead: Matt Burlage & Bill Nichol & Betty PunStatus: Not started

AI-enabled data exploration and business decision support, drawing on the Data Lake's Curated and Reporting snapshot zones. Provides a guided exploration layer where business questions (product mix, channel profitability, scenario impact, capital efficiency, etc.) can be investigated against the certified data the rest of Project Nexus produces. Shares a front-end / access surface with Governance and Compliance (the Governance-owned reporting marts).

  • AI-enabled data exploration -- guided exploration of Curated and Reporting snapshot data with business-decision framing.
  • Business decision support -- scenario analysis on top of Cashfluid / HKRBC / Sun outputs.
  • FP&A capability (future) -- driver-based budgeting, rolling forecasts, scenario planning (currently Appendix A row 15, Future). Likely lands inside Strategy when scoped, sharing infrastructure with the exploration layer.

Pillar 2 (ORSA governance, stress orchestration) and Pillar 3 (public disclosure templates) placement remains an OPEN QUESTION across Governance / Compliance / Strategy.

4. Data Lake -- Architecture and Functions

Status. The bullets below are preliminary, with the more recent update being Michael's images attached. Michael will walk us through the Data Lake architecture design on the May 12 2026 meeting and adjust this section accordingly.

Allocation of the specific Lake Functions within the Data Lake (see Section 4.5 and the Open Question in Section 2) is the most pressing issue that needs to be clearly agreed to and assigned for development, specifically with regards to which ones will be done by Coreflexi in the interim (reporting period rush) and which will be developed by the Data Lake team.

4.1 Architecture

Five foundational architectural choices set the platform, the layer model, the documentation surface, and how external feeds reach Nexus:

  • Platform: MySQL-first curated analytics platform. The Data Lake runs on a MySQL-first stack with Python pipelines and GitLab as the home for SQL, Python jobs, mappings, MDM definitions, Wiki pages, changelogs and release tags. The Project Nexus modules (Cashfluid, Sun 2.x, Moody's 2.x, HKRBC Reporting, Transactional, Governance) sit on the same MySQL platform but in their own schemas / databases for specialist logic and module-owned state, while the shared Lake schemas hold certified cross-module datasets, MDM, reference data and immutable snapshots. Module logic is Python, consuming from MySQL and writing back through controlled interfaces. An Azure migration remains an explicit future option, triggered only when MySQL storage / query concurrency, semi-structured data volume, or orchestration complexity actually justify it -- not as a default starting point.
  • Layer model: four zones. Data progresses through four zones: Raw (faithful landing of source extracts; CDC, external feeds, module outputs as received; no business reinterpretation), Standardised (typed, cleaned, deduplicated, conformed; code lists harmonised; source-system noise removed without injecting specialist meaning), Curated (certified reusable business-ready datasets; mastered entities, shared dimensions, validated facts; the zone modules and Governance consume from), and Reporting snapshot (immutable, versioned, audit-stamped evidence behind every published report -- the official data used for a specific submission (e.g. a final HKRBC return) so every figure can be reproduced and reconciled back to source).
  • PACE GitHub repo -- shared wiki home. The PACE GitHub repo will host the Data Lake Data Wiki and the Nexus Projects wiki. Between them they will hold all the memory frameworks of our builds and projects, providing shared context for every Claude Code agent on the programme. (The Data Lake Data Wiki is separate from the Coreflexi Data Wiki described in card 1, but will reference it for daily data updates and will check that any Core Admin DB schema or structure changes are properly reflected in the Coreflexi Data Wiki.)
  • External data: two flows, one core rule. Core rule: no external source has direct read or write access to a Nexus-controlled database. External providers never get a connection string to Coreflexi, the Data Lake, or any module store. All external data enters Nexus via one of two controlled channels:
    • Flow 1 -- Direct-to-Module Feed (professional / API channel). For external sources that publish via a stable API or professionally-structured feed where quality and schema are guaranteed at source (asset-manager real-world projections, market data, regulator-published reference rates, capital-market assumptions). The consuming Nexus module owns the inbound adapter and the schema validation against the source's contract; data flows directly into the module, bypassing the Lake. Chosen for build efficiency (consuming module authors its own connector against a stable contract) and processing efficiency (no Lake round-trip for data that is already curated at source). Some module runs may require this connection on-demand for a single run.
    • Flow 2 -- Ingestion Module → Data Lake → Consuming Modules. For external data that is ad hoc, multi-consumer, or quality-variable (regulatory documents, experience studies, sanctions / PEP lists, ESG datasets, third-party reference tables). The data passes through a dedicated External Data Ingestion Module (Section 4.6) that lands it in the Lake's Raw zone, validates and standardises it, and publishes it to Curated for downstream consumption by any number of Nexus modules. This guarantees a single extraction-and-quality layer -- independent of any individual module build -- with versioning, lineage, and reproducibility across cross-module use.
    Routing decision (per dataset): if the source provides a stable contract and only one or a small handful of modules consume it, use Flow 1; if the source is ad-hoc or the dataset is reused across multiple modules, use Flow 2.
  • PII masking on the Core Admin DB → Lake sync. The Core Admin DB holds personally identifiable information; the Data Lake does not. The one-way daily sync (CDC / batch) masks PII en route. Any downstream module that needs raw PII accesses it directly from the Core Admin DB through an authorised channel -- it cannot be sourced from the Lake.

4.2 Master Data Management (MDM)

The MDM plan is the governing document for everything the Data Lake holds. It must clearly cover all of the structural contents below. The MDM is consumed by the Data Wiki (Section 4.3) and is updated whenever data methodology changes.

  • Purpose and scope -- what the MDM governs, where it applies, and what it explicitly excludes.
  • Master data domains -- the canonical entities (preliminary): Customer, Policy, Product, Counterparty, Chart of Accounts mapping, Currency, Calendar; plus any further domains added during build.
  • Systems of record -- for each domain / entity, the designated mastering module and the modules permitted to read or contribute attributes.
  • Canonical data model -- attribute-level definitions, data types, mandatory vs. optional fields, relationships between entities.
  • Business glossary -- business-language definitions for every entity and key attribute, owned by the relevant business stakeholder.
  • Governance roles -- data owners, data stewards, custodians, approvers; named accountable individuals per domain.
  • Data quality rules -- validation rules, completeness / accuracy / consistency / timeliness checks, with thresholds and SLAs.
  • Hierarchy and mapping management -- reference hierarchies (product taxonomy, organisational hierarchy, CoA), code-list mappings, and cross-system mapping tables.
  • Source-system attribute mappings -- for each source system (Coreflexi, external feeds, module outputs), the field-level mapping from source-system attribute to the canonical entity attribute and onward to the journal-entry attribute downstream. Includes data-type transformation, unit / format normalisation, and the system-of-record reference.
  • Economic trigger points and event finalisation -- for each economic event (premium, claim, surrender, commission, lapse, etc.), the source-system event that signals the event has become final for downstream processing, and the workflow step at which that finalisation occurs (e.g. premium-billed vs premium-received vs premium-earned; claim-notified vs claim-reserved vs claim-paid; surrender-requested vs surrender-approved vs surrender-paid). Drives which posting rule fires, what attribute set is available at trigger time, and the lineage from source event to downstream JE.
  • Versioning and effective dating -- how reference data and canonical entities are versioned, how effective-from / effective-to dating is applied, and how historical states are retrievable.
  • Change approval workflow -- the process for proposing, reviewing, approving and publishing changes to canonical entities, hierarchies, or data quality rules.
  • Data Lake zones and certification rules -- which zones (Raw / Standardised / Curated / Reporting snapshot -- see Section 4.1) hold what data, and the criteria for promoting data between zones.
  • Lineage and metadata requirements -- end-to-end traceability from source-system field through every transformation to the consuming system or report; technical and business metadata captured for every dataset.
  • Security and access controls -- role-based access, masking of sensitive attributes (PII / PDPO), segregation of duties, audit logging of access.
  • Reconciliation controls -- how data in the Data Lake is reconciled back to source systems and forward to consuming systems; reconciliation cadence and break-resolution procedure.
  • Issue management -- how data-quality breaches and data-related incidents are logged, triaged, escalated and remediated.
  • Audit and compliance requirements -- evidence captured to satisfy internal audit, external audit, and regulatory examination (HKIA, PDPO, etc.).
  • Operating metrics and KPIs -- the metrics by which MDM effectiveness is measured (data-quality scores, reconciliation pass rate, change cycle time, incident counts, etc.).
  • Lake Functions Registry -- tracks the Lake Functions themselves (definition, owner, consuming modules, status, change history).

4.3 Data Wiki -- documentation layer

  • Owner: Michael Alo -- responsible for building the Wiki and for proposing the maintenance model (ownership, review cadence, change-control workflow that ties methodology changes to MDM updates).
  • Core Admin DB documentation: Kai Wang -- to be incorporated into the Wiki and maintained separately.
  • The Wiki surfaces MDM definitions and must make explicit that any change in data methodology requires a corresponding update to the MDM.
  • The Wiki is intended to be consumed by all Claude Code agents on this programme so every agent operates with the same definitions of data sources, canonical entities, and transformation logic. This makes the Wiki a load-bearing artefact, not a passive reference site.

The PACE GitHub repo will host the Data Lake Data Wiki and the Nexus Projects wiki.

4.4 Consumers of the Curated and Reporting snapshot zones

  • Modules -- each Project Nexus module reads Standardised / Curated datasets as input and writes its outputs back into Curated through controlled interfaces (see Section 5 inputs / outputs columns).
  • Governance -- reads Curated outputs for compliance monitoring, attestations, and other oversight workflows.
  • Reporting marts CONFIRMED -- reporting marts live in Governance as the single front-end / access surface from which final reports are downloaded (statutory packs, management reporting, HKFRS17 disclosure mart, HKRBC dashboards, board / ALCO packs). The underlying reporting-data snapshots (the official data used for each published submission, e.g. a final HKRBC return) live in the Data Lake's Reporting snapshot zone (Section 4.1) so every figure can be reproduced and reconciled back to source. This gives a clean split: Governance owns the front-end and workflow context; the Data Lake owns the certified, versioned, reproducible data behind every published result.

4.5 Lake Functions -- manipulations performed by the Data Lake (preliminary)

[to be amended as data requirements are updated]

  • Ingestion (Core Admin DB and module outputs) -- CDC / batch from Core Admin DB, system outputs from each Nexus module.
  • External-data ingestion adapters (Flow 2) -- Raw landing, schema validation, standardisation, Curated promotion, versioning and lineage for external datasets consumed across multiple Nexus modules (regulatory documents, experience studies, sanctions lists, ESG reference, etc.). Owned by the External Data Ingestion Module (Section 4.6).
  • Standardisation & deduplication -- format normalisation, duplicate resolution, code-list harmonisation against MDM reference data.
  • MDM survivorship -- golden-record assembly across sources (e.g. Customer master from multiple onboarding channels).
  • Aggregation -- to reporting cells, HKFRS17 contract groups, GL lines, regulatory templates.
  • Re-routing -- delivering Standardised / Curated datasets to each Nexus module as inputs.
  • Lineage capture -- every transformation tracked end-to-end so every reported number can be traced back to source.
  • Data quality -- validation rules, reconciliations, observability, and quality SLAs by domain.
  • Reference data versioning -- versioned code lists, taxonomies, effective-dating.
  • Snapshotting -- point-in-time snapshots for reporting, audit, and re-runs.
  • Access governance -- role-based access, masking of sensitive fields, PDPO compliance.
  • Lake Functions -- module specific functions required by each system. See Section 5 for initial processes.

The working principle proposed below (subject to refinement with each module owner) for the separation of Lake Function and Module Function:

  • Lake Function (default for generic data work). Anything that is generic data engineering / governance and could in principle be reused across multiple modules: ingestion, format normalisation, deduplication, MDM survivorship, code-list harmonisation, reference-data versioning, snapshotting, lineage capture, data quality validation, access governance, and aggregations or decompositions whose logic is generic (e.g. sum-by-key, time-bucketing). As mentioned above, there are currently some finance data taggings / functions that are performed in Coreflexi by Luke. These and other immediate-need functions (for reporting deadlines) should continue to be performed / built in Coreflexi.
  • Module Function (specialist domain logic). Anything that requires actuarial, accounting, regulatory, compliance, or transactional specialist business logic, e.g. liability cash-flow projection (Cashfluid), journal validation against the CoA (Sun 2.x), CSM roll-forward presentation (Moody's 2.x), Pillar 1 capital computation (HKRBC Reporting), portfolio carve-outs (Transactional), attestation workflow (Governance).
  • Tie-breaker for the grey area. Where a function could go either way (most often aggregation): if the aggregation rule is generic, place it in the Lake; if it depends on specialist domain definitions (e.g. HKFRS17 contract groups, RBC capital tiers), place it in the relevant module. Where two modules would each implement the same thing, default to a Lake Function.
Task: Michael please amend this section as necessary.

4.6 External Data Ingestion Module New deliverable

Owner: Data Lake team (Shanshan Gu, Michael Alo, Luke Lai)Status: New deliverable -- to be folded into the Data Lake architecture and MDM

The External Data Ingestion Module is the Lake-side counterpart to Flow 2 in Section 4.1. It is the single, controlled entry point through which ad-hoc and multi-consumer external data enters Nexus. It guarantees that no external provider ever touches a Nexus-controlled database directly, and that any dataset consumed by more than one module is curated, versioned, and quality-assured exactly once -- not re-implemented by every consuming module.

Scope of datasets it serves (initial wave). The Ingestion Module is the route for datasets where (a) structure or quality varies by source, (b) multiple Nexus modules consume the same dataset, or (c) the data is reusable reference material rather than transient runtime input. Initial-wave list:

  • HKIA returns templates (regulatory templates used by HKRBC Reporting and Compliance)
  • IFRS 17 / HKRBC regulatory guidance text (interpreted by Moody's 2.x, HKRBC Reporting, Compliance)
  • SOA mortality and lapse experience studies (consumed by Cashfluid and HKRBC Reporting)
  • Reinsurer experience tables (Cashfluid, Transactional)
  • Sanctions and PEP lists (Compliance, Governance, Transactional)
  • ESG reference datasets (Strategy, Cashfluid for scenario calibration)

Boundary with Flow 1 (direct-to-Module). The Ingestion Module is not a catch-all for every external feed. Flow 1 remains the right pattern for sources that publish a stable API / structured feed with guaranteed quality and schema at source, where a single Nexus module (or a small handful) consumes it inside its own runtime -- e.g., asset-manager real-world projections into Cashfluid, market data feeds for SAA runs, regulator-published reference rates consumed at run-time. Routing each dataset to Flow 1 vs Flow 2 is a per-dataset decision recorded in the MDM. The default heuristic: if the consumer count is one and the source already certifies the data, prefer Flow 1; otherwise prefer Flow 2.

What the Ingestion Module does (operationally). Lands the source payload in the Lake's Raw zone; runs Lake Functions (schema validation, standardisation, deduplication, code-list harmonisation against MDM, quality rules); promotes the curated dataset to Curated with semver and effective dates; captures lineage so any downstream consumer can trace a value back to the source payload; enforces access governance (PDPO masking for any sensitive datasets). It is, in operating terms, a specialised Flow-2 instance of the Lake Functions enumerated in Section 4.5 -- the Data Lake team will fold the detailed design (sources, adapter contracts, dataset-by-dataset MDM entries, refresh cadence) into the broader Data Lake architecture and MDM deliverable owned by Shanshan and Michael.

5. Data dependencies -- inputs, outputs, manipulations

Convention used in this section. Each module's external-data inputs are tagged (Flow 1 direct) or (Flow 2 via Ingestion Module) per the routing rules in Section 4.1. Flow 1 = professional / API channel directly into the module; Flow 2 = ingestion via the External Data Ingestion Module (Section 4.6) into the Data Lake, then to consumers. Where not yet tagged, the default routing should be decided with the Data Lake team as part of the MDM.

5.3 Cashfluid

Module project plan -- attached: three files embedded below; click to download. Phase A and Phase B are treated as complete; the forward plan covers Lake integration, SAA / ALM, AI onboarding, assumption authoring, and FE.

From Governance (pre-Governance-live = direct from board / risk function):

  • Structured ORSA scenarios (Phase 2 Step s, deferred)

From External Data Sources:

  • In-house RN-ESG codebase (Phase 2 Step f) -- (Flow 1 direct)
  • Asset-manager standard metric conventions + proxy asset curves (Phase 2 Steps l, m) -- (Flow 1 direct)
  • Real-world asset projections (from asset managers, future) -- (Flow 1 direct)

  • Legacy product documentation (PDF / CSV / Excel) and legacy 5 / 1,000 / portfolio parity outputs (Phase 3 Steps d, k, m, n) -- (Flow 2 via Ingestion Module)
From Coreflexi 2.x:
  • FE GitLab repo (Wenbo) -- one-time reusability audit (Phase 1 Step a)
 Foundations (Phase A & B complete):
  • RunSpec + multi-tenant / per-acquisition-block isolation
  • PolicyModelPoint schema + pluggable DataConnector Protocol (CSV today, Lake later)
  • Assumption framework: SchemaMetadata, AssumptionTable + interpolation, lookups, GovernanceWorkflow

Phase 2 -- Model Running & ALM:
  • CapitalConstraint hierarchy: PCRStub -> PCROnly -> PCRPlusPillar2Scalar -> PCRPlusPillar2Computed -> PCRPlusAccountingReserve -> EconomicCapital (ADR-031)

  • SAA optimisation loop: mean-variance -> stochastic; RN-ESG wrap; Reserve Requirement engine
  • HKRBC-INT consumption client (stub then live); par dividend recompute
  • Convergence orchestrator (damped fixed-point; non-convergence escalation per ADR-032)
  • Daily SAA scheduler (cron-style; M3 MVP)
  • Asset-curve schema + liability selector + KRD matching engine (LP / QP)
Phase 3 -- AI Integration:
  • YAML product SDK + State Machine framework; RAG knowledge base; document parsing pipeline
  • AI codegen + parity automation (tolerance + classification per ADR-033)
  • Assisted onboarding (10-stage flow with chief actuary in the loop; M5)
  • From-scratch design (archetype-driven wizard; M6)
Phase 4 -- Assumption Authoring:

  • External adapter framework (iPACE / Flow 2: ingestion may migrate to the External Data Ingestion Module under Section 4.6 -- see Phase 4 plan)

Phase 1 -- Front End:

  • Reporting marts (HKRBC dashboards, capital ratio monitoring, board / ALCO packs)

5.8 Governance

Sub-domainInputs (from)Proposed Module processesProposed Data Lake processesOutputs (to)
1. Risk & Capital Governance
  • Cashfluid (cash flows, ESG scenarios, stress runs)
  • HKRBC Reporting (PCR / MCR, MOCE, sensitivity outputs)
  • Sun 2.x (financials by book)
  • Model inventory feeds from each module (Cashfluid, HKRBC, Moody's)
  • Internal incident / breach data (Lake Standardised)
  • Risk Register + Risk Appetite Statement maintenance
  • ORSA governance and narrative assembly (Pillar 2 placement OPEN QUESTION)
  • Model Risk Management (inventory, validation cadence, sign-off)
  • Capital Management Policy oversight
  • Stress testing governance (scenarios, calendar, approvals)
  • Ingestion of cross-module risk + capital data
  • Snapshotting of stress scenarios and model runs
  • Lineage from model output through validation evidence
  • MDM for Risk and Model entities
  • Risk Register and Model Risk register (to Lake)
  • ORSA artefacts (to HKIA via Compliance flow)
  • Model validation approvals (signals back to Cashfluid, HKRBC, Moody's 2.x)
  • KRI dashboards (via Reporting marts)
2. Conduct & Ethics
  • HR records (training, F&P attestations, staff conduct events)
  • Coreflexi customer interactions and complaints feeds
  • Whistleblower reports (manual intake)
  • Breach data from across modules
  • Staff Conduct (Code of Conduct, Fit & Proper, training & attestations)
  • Conduct / breach workflow
  • Whistleblowing register
  • Anti-bribery / Gifts & Entertainment register
  • Complaints handling oversight
  • Ingestion of HR + complaints data
  • MDM for Staff entity
  • Access governance for sensitive conduct / whistleblower data
  • Snapshotting of incidents and dispositions
  • Whistleblowing register and Anti-bribery register (to Lake)
  • Conduct / breach disposition records
  • Complaints handling outputs (back to Coreflexi for customer-side closure)
  • Staff conduct attestations
3. Operational Governance
  • Policy library
  • Procurement requests (from across modules) + vendor data
  • BCP / DR test results
  • Security incident data
  • Internal audit findings
  • Policy / Procedure Management
  • DOA (Delegation of Authority) & approval matrices
  • Procurement governance (policy, vendor due diligence)
  • Third-party / outsourcing register
  • BCP / DR governance
  • Information Security governance (cyber, distinct from PDPO)
  • MDM for Policy, Vendor, Outsourcing entities
  • Access governance for sensitive operational evidence
  • Snapshotting of policy versions and DOA matrices
  • Policy Register, Vendor / Outsourcing Register (to Lake)
  • DOA matrices (consumed by every module for workflow approvals)
  • BCP / DR test evidence; InfoSec controls evidence
4. Board & Committee Governance
  • Reporting marts (financials, capital, risk, conduct)
  • Sun 2.x and HKRBC Reporting outputs
  • Risk Register and ORSA artefacts (from sub-domain 1)
  • Director records (HR side)
  • Committee charters & calendars (Board, Audit, Risk, Par, Investment)
  • Board reporting pack assembly
  • Resolutions register
  • Director appointments & training
  • Attestations / sign-offs
  • Snapshotting of board-pack data sets (audit trail)
  • MDM for Resolutions and Director entities
  • Access governance
  • Board / committee packs (to Reporting marts front-end)
  • Resolutions Register, Director Register (to Lake)
  • Signed attestations (audit trail)
5. Audit & Attestation Backbone (cross-cutting; supports Governance, Compliance and Strategy)
  • Evidence from sub-domains 1-4 plus Compliance and Strategy modules
  • System logs across all Nexus modules
  • Transaction-level data from all modules (via Lake Standardised)
  • External auditor working-paper requirements
  • Internal audit coordination
  • External audit support
  • Attestation evidence repository
  • End-to-end lineage capture across all modules
  • Immutable evidence storage (audit trail Lake Function)
  • Access governance for audit / regulator queries
  • Audit working papers / evidence packs (to internal and external auditors)
  • Attestation registers (to Lake)
  • Audit findings tracking (consumed by every module for remediation)

5.9 Compliance

Sub-domainInputs (from)Proposed Module processesProposed Data Lake processesOutputs (to)
1. Regulatory Compliance
  • External regulatory feeds (HKIA GLs, CIRs, IIC papers; HKFRS updates)
  • Tax tables and statutory deadlines
  • Licence registers (HKIA insurer, ICB, HKFI, intermediaries)
  • Reporting marts (financials, capital, conduct data)
  • Sun 2.x (financial-statement inputs for statutory filings)
  • Obligation Register (HKIA, MPL-specific, undertaking deliverables)
  • Licence management
  • Monthly Attestation Program
  • Companies Ordinance filings (Annual Return, SCR, AGM, statutory books)
  • Tax compliance oversight (profits tax, employer's return, agent / broker return)
  • Regulatory horizon scanning
  • AML / CFT policy oversight
  • Ingestion of regulatory text feeds and statutory deadlines
  • Structured-data extraction from regulatory text into obligation entities
  • MDM for Obligation Register and Licence Register
  • Reference-data versioning for regulatory text
  • Obligation Register, Licence Register, AML/CFT policy register (to Lake)
  • Statutory filings (Companies Registry, IRD)
  • Monthly attestation evidence
2. Data & Privacy
  • Customer PII flows (Coreflexi → Lake)
  • Data retention schedules (regulatory)
  • Sanctions / PEP lists (external)
  • Lake access logs and data-quality breach reports
  • PDPO / data privacy oversight
  • Data retention / records management
  • Sanctions / PEP screening governance
  • Access governance Lake Function operationalises PDPO policy
  • Ingestion of sanctions / PEP lists
  • MDM for Customer (with PII attributes flagged)
  • Lineage capture for data subject access requests
  • PDPO controls evidence
  • Data retention policy
  • Sanctions / PEP Register (to Lake)
  • Access policy back to Lake (enforcement loop)
3. RPT register
  • Definition of related-party criteria (HKFRS 24, HKIA expectations)
  • Intra-group entity register (PSL, MPL, planned IoM entity)
  • Director / KMP register (from Governance Board & Committee sub-domain)
  • RPT register maintenance (related-party definitions, approval workflow)
  • Transfer-pricing documentation oversight
  • MDM for Counterparty entity (with is_related_party attribute)
  • Lake's data-tagging service applies the RPT flag to every JE line via Counterparty lookup
  • RPT Register (to Lake)
  • RPT dimension on every Sun 2.x JE line (carved out for disclosure)
  • RPT disclosure submissions to HKIA (per sub-domain 4)
4. Non-Pillar-1 HKIA returns
  • HKRBC Reporting (Pillar-1 capital figures)
  • Sun 2.x (financials)
  • Cashfluid (actuarial figures)
  • RPT register (from sub-domain 3)
  • Risk and ORSA artefacts (from Governance sub-domain 1)
  • Quarterly Management Report composition
  • Annual Business Plan composition
  • RPT submission composition
  • Filing workflow and attestation
  • Reporting marts consolidate data across Sun, HKRBC, Cashfluid for composite returns
  • Snapshotting of submitted returns in Reporting snapshot zone
  • HKIA non-Pillar-1 submissions
  • Attestation evidence (to Lake)

Pillar 2 (ORSA governance, stress orchestration) and Pillar 3 (public disclosure templates) placement remains an OPEN QUESTION across Governance / Compliance / Strategy.

5.10 Strategy

Sub-domainInputs (from)Proposed Module processesProposed Data Lake processesOutputs (to)
1. AI-enabled data exploration
  • Curated zone (all certified cross-module datasets)
  • Reporting snapshot zone (immutable evidence behind published reports)
  • MDM golden records and reference data
  • Lake's Data Wiki (definitions, lineage, business glossary)
  • Guided AI exploration of Lake data with business-decision framing (product mix, channel profitability, capital efficiency, scenario impact)
  • Conversational interface to query certified data with lineage-aware answers
  • Lake provides governed read access via MDM and access governance
  • Lineage capture from exploration query back to underlying source data
  • Exploration outputs (analyses, decision memos) saved back into Curated where worth retaining
  • Front-end / access surface shared with Governance and Compliance reporting marts
2. Business decision support
  • Module outputs (Cashfluid scenarios, HKRBC capital sensitivities, Sun 2.x financials, Transactional deal models)
  • External benchmarks (where available)
  • Scenario analysis on top of module outputs
  • Cross-module decision support (e.g. capital impact of a portfolio decision; expense impact of a product launch)
  • Snapshotting of scenario inputs and outputs for audit trail
  • Lineage from scenario back to module sources
  • Decision memos and scenario outputs (to Lake)
  • Signals back to module owners when scenarios suggest configuration changes
3. FP&A capability (future scope)
  • Sun 2.x actuals
  • Cashfluid projections
  • HKRBC capital projections
  • Business assumptions (manual + Lake-stored)
  • Driver-based budgeting
  • Rolling forecast
  • Scenario planning
  • Reference-data versioning for budgets and forecasts
  • Snapshotting of plan versions
  • Budget / forecast / plan datasets (to Lake)
  • Variance reports to Reporting marts

Boundaries with Governance (Risk & Capital sub-domain) and Compliance (regulatory analytics) are recorded as OPEN QUESTIONs in Section 2.

6. Next steps

  1. Project plans by module owner. Each module lead drafts a phased project plan for their module, anchored to the inputs / outputs / processes in Section 5 (and to be refined as needed). Plans must include the data each phase needs (and when) and the outputs each phase produces (and when). Target completion: Monday 18 May 2026. The Cashfluid project plan (see project_plans/Cashfluid/) is the structural reference (not a template -- each module's phasing will look different).
  2. Review of proposed Data Lake architecture and MDM -- Shanshan Gu & Luke Lai. Shanshan Gu and Luke Lai to review and make edits to the proposed Data Lake architecture and MDM authored by Michael Alo.
  3. Final allocation of Lake Functions vs Module Functions. Once project plans (item 1) are in, produce the definitive per-module list of Lake Functions vs Module Functions, building on the working principle in Section 4.5 and the sub-bullets in Section 2. Assign development tasks for the Lake Functions accordingly.
  4. Detailed data-flow system architecture -- Matt Burlage. Matt Burlage to produce a more detailed pure dataflow document on top of Michael's data architecture (item 2) and the module project plans (item 1), replacing the preliminary Section 1 diagram with a full input-to-output data lineage view.
  5. Master Data Management plan. Data Lake team to finalize first draft of the MDM plan by Friday 22 May 2026.
  6. External Data Ingestion Module -- Data Lake team. Incorporate the External Data Ingestion Module (Section 4.6) into the Data Lake architecture and MDM. Define source-by-source routing (Flow 1 vs Flow 2), adapter contracts, refresh cadence, and the initial-wave dataset list (HKIA returns templates, IFRS 17 / HKRBC guidance text, SOA experience studies, reinsurer tables, sanctions lists, ESG reference).

Document version: v1.
